doom.driver: U.S. to dismantle system tracking Atlantic currents that are at risk of collapse.
doom.blurb: BLIND TO THE CONVEYOR BELT OF DOOM
doom.summary: The Trump administration is dismantling the ocean monitoring system that tracks AMOC — the Atlantic current whose collapse would redraw agriculture, weather, and coastlines across the Northern Hemisphere. We're not just ignoring the warning light; we're ripping it out of the dashboard. Meanwhile, Japan is running short of plastic bags because Iran's war disrupted naphtha supplies, which is a preview of how civilizational unraveling actually feels: mundane, cascading, and weird.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: Iran-Gulf tensions persist — Kuwait's airport hit by drones again — even as an Israel-Lebanon ceasefire framework offers a rare flicker of de-escalation.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: Dismantling AMOC monitoring infrastructure is a governance failure with multi-century consequences; the US House's symbolic Iran war rebuke signals real but toothless discord.

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Gemma 4 12B runs on any laptop with 16GB RAM and AI-generated lawsuits are flooding federal courts — capability diffusion accelerating faster than legal frameworks can absorb.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: AI leaders publish open letter calling for tougher bioweapon safeguards, implicitly acknowledging the current guardrails are inadequate.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Flesh-eating screwworm confirmed crossing the US-Mexico border into South Texas — a slow-moving biological threat that wasn't on most people's bingo cards.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — extreme close-up filling the entire frame with a crumpled scientific journal or oceanographic report lying on a dark wet surface, camera pressed flat and looking straight down, the pages soaked and curling at the edges, deep navy shadows bleeding across the paper, a detailed diagram of the Atlantic meridional overturning circulation visible on the open page — concentric arrows showing the great current loop from tropics to Greenland rendered in faded teal and orange ink — the paper punctured by a single bureaucratic hole-punch in the upper corner, a torn NSF funding notice partially overlapping it, the institutional seal barely legible through water damage, the smoldering orange of a nearby work lamp reflecting off the wet surface in broken pixel fragments, barnacle-encrusted rope coiled at the extreme edge of the frame, the entire composition drowning in heavy shadow with only the circulation diagram catching the threatening amber light, implying vast cold ocean darkness beyond the document's edges.

dawn.driver: Missing Sherpa guide found on Mount Everest after 'miracle' self-rescue. Cleaners found Dawa Sherpa crawling towards Base Camp six days after he went missing at a higher altitude.
dawn.blurb: SIX DAYS ALONE IN THE DEATH ZONE — AND HE WALKED OUT
dawn.summary: Against a backdrop of wars, drone strikes, and geopolitical brinkmanship, Dawa Sherpa's survival on Everest is a stunning reminder of what the human body and will can endure. Missing for six days at extreme altitude — no food, no water, no supplemental oxygen — he was found crawling toward Base Camp under his own power, while his family had already begun funeral rites. It's a small story in the grand ledger of today's news, but it carries something the headlines rarely offer: proof that people can survive the unsurvivable.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Dawa Sherpa's six-day survival in Everest's death zone without food, water, or oxygen, and his self-rescue, defies medical expectation and stands as a rare beacon of resilience.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: Israel and Lebanon have agreed to a ceasefire framework in US-led talks, a fragile but real diplomatic signal amid an otherwise escalating regional conflict.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: NASA's James Webb Space Telescope detected methane on interstellar comet 3I/ATLAS — the first such detection on a visitor from another star system, expanding our understanding of interstellar chemistry.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: India's monsoon rains reached Kerala — three days late but in time to protect harvests and support the nation's $4 trillion economy through another growing season.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Cancer's own immune evasion trick may be its undoing — new research overturns a core immunology belief and opens a surprising new avenue for killing tumor cells.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Silhouette composition, strong backlight, dramatic. The Khumbu Icefall on Mount Everest at golden hour, towering seracs and fractured glacial blocks rendered in deep teal and shadow-blue rising on all sides, the iconic Everest summit pyramid visible in the far upper background with a wind-blown snow plume streaming from its peak, the entire scene backlit by a blazing amber and gold horizon sky above Base Camp visible as tiny warm-colored tent specks glowing in the far distance below. In the foreground, a lone figure in a down suit crawls forward across the ice in full silhouette, body low and exhausted but pointed toward the light, a backpack still strapped to his back, an ice axe dragging beside him, a long groove carved into the snow behind him stretching back into darkness showing the distance already crossed alone. Two smaller silhouetted figures stand further down the slope, one arm raised and pointing toward the crawling man, their bodies frozen mid-stride in shock. Warm amber sidelight catches the ice crystal edges in scattered gold sparks, cold deep teal shadows fill the crevasses, the contrast between deadly blue environment and warm golden backlight making the crawling survivor glow as if lit from within.
